solution:
Solution 1. We will show that \( c=|\ln (a / b)| \) by proving that the inequality is satisfied if and only if \( 0<|u| \leq|\ln (a / b)| \). The right-hand side is an even function of \( u \); hence it suffices to consider \( u>0 \). Replacing \( x \) by \( 1-x \) and interchanging \( a \) and \( b \) preserves the inequality, so we may assume \( a \geq b \).

We will show that for each \( x \in(0,1) \), the function
\[
F(u)=a \frac{\sinh u x}{\sinh u}+b \frac{\sinh u(1-x)}{\sinh u}-a^{x} b^{1-x}
\]
of \( u \) is decreasing for \( u>0 \). For this, it suffices to show that for each \( \alpha \in(0,1) \), the function
\[
f(u)=\frac{\sinh \alpha u}{\sinh u}
\]
is decreasing for \( u>0 \), since \( F(u) \) is a constant plus a sum of positive multiples of two such functions \( f \). Differentiating with respect to \( u \) yields
\[
f^{\prime}(u)=\frac{\alpha \cosh (\alpha u) \sinh u-\sinh (\alpha u) \cosh u}{\sinh ^{2} u}
\]
which for \( u>0 \) is negative if and only if the value of the function
\[
g(u)=\alpha \tanh u-\tanh (\alpha u)
\]
is negative. The negativity of \( g(u) \) for \( u>0 \) follows from \( g(0)=0 \) and the negativity of
\[
g^{\prime}(u)=\frac{\alpha}{\cosh ^{2} u}-\frac{\alpha}{\cosh ^{2} \alpha u}
\]
which in turn follows since cosh is increasing on \( \mathbb{R}^{+} \). Thus \( F(u) \) is decreasing for \( u>0 \).

If \( a>b \) then \( F(u) \) is zero at \( u=\ln (a / b) \). If \( a=b \) then \( \lim _{u \rightarrow 0^{+}} F(u)=0 \) by L'Hopital's Rule [Spv, Ch. 11, Theorem 9]. Since \( F(u) \) is decreasing for \( u>0 \), in both cases we have \( F(u) \geq 0 \) for \( 0<u \leq \ln (a / b) \) and \( F(u)<0 \) for \( u>\ln (a / b) \), as desired.

Solution 2. As before, we may assume \( u>0 \) and \( a \geq b \). Taking \( \ln \) of each side and rearranging, we find that the inequality is equivalent to
\[
x(\ln (a \sinh u))+(1-x)(\ln (b \sinh u)) \leq \ln (a \sinh u x+b \sinh u(1-x)),
\]
which, if we define
\[
f(x)=a \sinh u x+b \sinh u(1-x),
\]
can be written as
\[
x \ln f(1)+(1-x) \ln f(0) \leq \ln f(x)
\]

This will hold if \( u \) is such that \( \ln f(x) \) is concave for \( x \in(0,1) \), and will fail if \( \ln f(x) \) is strictly convex on \( (0,1) \).

We compute
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d}{d x} \ln f= & \frac{f^{\prime}}{f} \\
\frac{d^{2}}{d x^{2}} \ln f= & \frac{f f^{\prime \prime}-\left(f^{\prime}\right)^{2}}{f^{2}} \\
f^{\prime}= & a u \cosh u x-b u \cosh u(1-x) \\
f^{\prime \prime}= & a u^{2} \sinh u x+b u^{2} \sinh u(1-x) \\
f f^{\prime \prime}-\left(f^{\prime}\right)^{2}= & \left(a^{2}+b^{2}\right) u^{2}\left(\sinh ^{2} u x-\cosh ^{2} u x\right) \\
& +2 a b u^{2}(\sinh u x \sinh u(1-x)+\cosh u x \cosh u(1-x)) \\
= & \left(a^{2}+b^{2}\right) u^{2}(-1)+2 a b u^{2} \cosh (u x+u(1-x)) \\
= & u^{2}\left(-a^{2}-b^{2}+2 a b \cosh u\right)
\end{aligned}
\]
o \( \frac{d^{2}}{d x^{2}} \ln f \) has constant sign for \( x \in(0,1) \), and the following are equivalent:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d^{2}}{d x^{2}} \ln f & \leq 0 \quad \text { for } x \in(0,1) \\
-a^{2}-b^{2}+2 a b \cosh u & \leq 0 \\
-a^{2}-b^{2}+a b e^{u}+a b e^{-u} & \leq 0 \\
-\left(a-e^{u} b\right)\left(a-e^{-u} b\right) & \leq 0 \\
-\left(a-e^{u} b\right) & \leq 0 \quad(\text { since } u>0 \text { and } a \geq b>0) \\
u & \leq \ln (a / b) .
\end{aligned}
\]

descriptive_long.solution:
Solution 1. We will show that \( largestc=|\ln (firstpos / secondpos)| \) by proving that the inequality is satisfied if and only if \( 0<|shiftvar| \leq|\ln (firstpos / secondpos)| \). The right-hand side is an even function of \( shiftvar \); hence it suffices to consider \( shiftvar>0 \). Replacing \( mixratio \) by \( 1-mixratio \) and interchanging \( firstpos \) and \( secondpos \) preserves the inequality, so we may assume \( firstpos \geq secondpos \).

We will show that for each \( mixratio \in(0,1) \), the function
\[
mainfun(shiftvar)=firstpos \frac{\sinh shiftvar mixratio}{\sinh shiftvar}+secondpos \frac{\sinh shiftvar(1-mixratio)}{\sinh shiftvar}-firstpos^{mixratio} secondpos^{1-mixratio}
\]
of \( shiftvar \) is decreasing for \( shiftvar>0 \). For this, it suffices to show that for each \( alphavar \in(0,1) \), the function
\[
smallfun(shiftvar)=\frac{\sinh alphavar \, shiftvar}{\sinh shiftvar}
\]
is decreasing for \( shiftvar>0 \), since \( mainfun(shiftvar) \) is a constant plus a sum of positive multiples of two such functions \( smallfun \). Differentiating with respect to \( shiftvar \) yields
\[
smallfun^{\prime}(shiftvar)=\frac{alphavar \cosh (alphavar \, shiftvar) \sinh shiftvar-\sinh (alphavar \, shiftvar) \cosh shiftvar}{\sinh ^{2} shiftvar}
\]
which for \( shiftvar>0 \) is negative if and only if the value of the function
\[
auxifun(shiftvar)=alphavar \tanh shiftvar-\tanh (alphavar \, shiftvar)
\]
is negative. The negativity of \( auxifun(shiftvar) \) for \( shiftvar>0 \) follows from \( auxifun(0)=0 \) and the negativity of
\[
auxifun^{\prime}(shiftvar)=\frac{alphavar}{\cosh ^{2} shiftvar}-\frac{alphavar}{\cosh ^{2} alphavar \, shiftvar}
\]
which in turn follows since cosh is increasing on \( \mathbb{R}^{+} \). Thus \( mainfun(shiftvar) \) is decreasing for \( shiftvar>0 \).

If \( firstpos>secondpos \) then \( mainfun(shiftvar) \) is zero at \( shiftvar=\ln (firstpos / secondpos) \). If \( firstpos=secondpos \) then \( \lim _{shiftvar \rightarrow 0^{+}} mainfun(shiftvar)=0 \) by L'Hopital's Rule [Spv, Ch. 11, Theorem 9]. Since \( mainfun(shiftvar) \) is decreasing for \( shiftvar>0 \), in both cases we have \( mainfun(shiftvar) \geq 0 \) for \( 0<shiftvar \leq \ln (firstpos / secondpos) \) and \( mainfun(shiftvar)<0 \) for \( shiftvar>\ln (firstpos / secondpos) \), as desired.

Solution 2. As before, we may assume \( shiftvar>0 \) and \( firstpos \geq secondpos \). Taking \( \ln \) of each side and rearranging, we find that the inequality is equivalent to
\[
mixratio(\ln (firstpos \sinh shiftvar))+(1-mixratio)(\ln (secondpos \sinh shiftvar)) \leq \ln (firstpos \sinh shiftvar \, mixratio+secondpos \sinh shiftvar(1-mixratio)),
\]
which, if we define
\[
smallfun(mixratio)=firstpos \sinh shiftvar \, mixratio+secondpos \sinh shiftvar(1-mixratio),
\]
can be written as
\[
mixratio \ln smallfun(1)+(1-mixratio) \ln smallfun(0) \leq \ln smallfun(mixratio)
\]

This will hold if \( shiftvar \) is such that \( \ln smallfun(mixratio) \) is concave for \( mixratio \in(0,1) \), and will fail if \( \ln smallfun(mixratio) \) is strictly convex on \( (0,1) \).

We compute
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d}{d mixratio} \ln smallfun= & \frac{smallfun^{\prime}}{smallfun} \\
\frac{d^{2}}{d mixratio^{2}} \ln smallfun= & \frac{smallfun \, smallfun^{\prime \prime}-\left(smallfun^{\prime}\right)^{2}}{smallfun^{2}} \\
smallfun^{\prime}= & firstpos \, shiftvar \cosh shiftvar \, mixratio-secondpos \, shiftvar \cosh shiftvar(1-mixratio) \\
smallfun^{\prime \prime}= & firstpos \, shiftvar^{2} \sinh shiftvar \, mixratio+secondpos \, shiftvar^{2} \sinh shiftvar(1-mixratio) \\
smallfun \, smallfun^{\prime \prime}-\left(smallfun^{\prime}\right)^{2}= & \left(firstpos^{2}+secondpos^{2}\right) shiftvar^{2}\left(\sinh ^{2} shiftvar \, mixratio-\cosh ^{2} shiftvar \, mixratio\right) \\
& +2 firstpos \, secondpos \, shiftvar^{2}(\sinh shiftvar \, mixratio \sinh shiftvar(1-mixratio)+\cosh shiftvar \, mixratio \cosh shiftvar(1-mixratio)) \\
= & \left(firstpos^{2}+secondpos^{2}\right) shiftvar^{2}(-1)+2 firstpos \, secondpos \, shiftvar^{2} \cosh (shiftvar \, mixratio+shiftvar(1-mixratio)) \\
= & shiftvar^{2}\left(-firstpos^{2}-secondpos^{2}+2 firstpos \, secondpos \cosh shiftvar\right)
\end{aligned}
\]
so \( \frac{d^{2}}{d mixratio^{2}} \ln smallfun \) has constant sign for \( mixratio \in(0,1) \), and the following are equivalent:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d^{2}}{d mixratio^{2}} \ln smallfun & \leq 0 \quad \text { for } mixratio \in(0,1) \\
-firstpos^{2}-secondpos^{2}+2 firstpos \, secondpos \cosh shiftvar & \leq 0 \\
-firstpos^{2}-secondpos^{2}+firstpos \, secondpos e^{shiftvar}+firstpos \, secondpos e^{-shiftvar} & \leq 0 \\
-\left(firstpos-e^{shiftvar} secondpos\right)\left(firstpos-e^{-shiftvar} secondpos\right) & \leq 0 \\
-\left(firstpos-e^{shiftvar} secondpos\right) & \leq 0 \quad(\text { since } shiftvar>0 \text { and } firstpos \geq secondpos>0) \\
shiftvar & \leq \ln (firstpos / secondpos) .
\end{aligned}
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution 1. We will show that \( driftwood=|\ln (lighthouse / snowflake)| \) by proving that the inequality is satisfied if and only if \( 0<|butterscotch| \leq|\ln (lighthouse / snowflake)| \). The right-hand side is an even function of \( butterscotch \); hence it suffices to consider \( butterscotch>0 \). Replacing \( honeycomb \) by \( 1-honeycomb \) and interchanging \( lighthouse \) and \( snowflake \) preserves the inequality, so we may assume \( lighthouse \geq snowflake \).

We will show that for each \( honeycomb \in(0,1) \), the function
\[
raspberries(butterscotch)=lighthouse \frac{\sinh butterscotch\, honeycomb}{\sinh butterscotch}+snowflake \frac{\sinh butterscotch(1-honeycomb)}{\sinh butterscotch}-lighthouse^{honeycomb} snowflake^{1-honeycomb}
\]
of \( butterscotch \) is decreasing for \( butterscotch>0 \). For this, it suffices to show that for each \( shipwreck \in(0,1) \), the function
\[
marshmallow(butterscotch)=\frac{\sinh shipwreck\, butterscotch}{\sinh butterscotch}
\]
is decreasing for \( butterscotch>0 \), since \( raspberries(butterscotch) \) is a constant plus a sum of positive multiples of two such functions \( marshmallow \). Differentiating with respect to \( butterscotch \) yields
\[
marshmallow^{\prime}(butterscotch)=\frac{shipwreck \cosh (shipwreck\, butterscotch) \sinh butterscotch-\sinh (shipwreck\, butterscotch) \cosh butterscotch}{\sinh ^{2} butterscotch}
\]
which for \( butterscotch>0 \) is negative if and only if the value of the function
\[
cheeseball(butterscotch)=shipwreck \tanh butterscotch-\tanh (shipwreck\, butterscotch)
\]
is negative. The negativity of \( cheeseball(butterscotch) \) for \( butterscotch>0 \) follows from \( cheeseball(0)=0 \) and the negativity of
\[
cheeseball^{\prime}(butterscotch)=\frac{shipwreck}{\cosh ^{2} butterscotch}-\frac{shipwreck}{\cosh ^{2} shipwreck\, butterscotch}
\]
which in turn follows since cosh is increasing on \( \mathbb{R}^{+} \). Thus \( raspberries(butterscotch) \) is decreasing for \( butterscotch>0 \).

If \( lighthouse>snowflake \) then \( raspberries(butterscotch) \) is zero at \( butterscotch=\ln (lighthouse / snowflake) \). If \( lighthouse=snowflake \) then \( \lim _{butterscotch \rightarrow 0^{+}} raspberries(butterscotch)=0 \) by L'H\^opital's Rule [Spv, Ch. 11, Theorem 9]. Since \( raspberries(butterscotch) \) is decreasing for \( butterscotch>0 \), in both cases we have \( raspberries(butterscotch) \geq 0 \) for \( 0<butterscotch \leq \ln (lighthouse / snowflake) \) and \( raspberries(butterscotch)<0 \) for \( butterscotch>\ln (lighthouse / snowflake) \), as desired.

Solution 2. As before, we may assume \( butterscotch>0 \) and \( lighthouse \geq snowflake \). Taking \( \ln \) of each side and rearranging, we find that the inequality is equivalent to
\[
honeycomb(\ln (lighthouse \sinh butterscotch))+(1-honeycomb)(\ln (snowflake \sinh butterscotch)) \leq \ln (lighthouse \sinh butterscotch\, honeycomb+snowflake \sinh butterscotch(1-honeycomb)),
\]
which, if we define
\[
marshmallow(honeycomb)=lighthouse \sinh butterscotch\, honeycomb+snowflake \sinh butterscotch(1-honeycomb),
\]
can be written as
\[
honeycomb \ln marshmallow(1)+(1-honeycomb) \ln marshmallow(0) \leq \ln marshmallow(honeycomb)
\]

This will hold if \( butterscotch \) is such that \( \ln marshmallow(honeycomb) \) is concave for \( honeycomb \in(0,1) \), and will fail if \( \ln marshmallow(honeycomb) \) is strictly convex on \( (0,1) \).

We compute
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d}{d honeycomb} \ln marshmallow=& \frac{marshmallow^{\prime}}{marshmallow} \\
\frac{d^{2}}{d honeycomb^{2}} \ln marshmallow=& \frac{marshmallow \, marshmallow^{\prime \prime}-\left(marshmallow^{\prime}\right)^{2}}{marshmallow^{2}} \\
marshmallow^{\prime}= & lighthouse \, butterscotch \cosh butterscotch\, honeycomb-snowflake \, butterscotch \cosh butterscotch(1-honeycomb) \\
marshmallow^{\prime \prime}= & lighthouse \, butterscotch^{2} \sinh butterscotch\, honeycomb+snowflake \, butterscotch^{2} \sinh butterscotch(1-honeycomb) \\
marshmallow \, marshmallow^{\prime \prime}-\left(marshmallow^{\prime}\right)^{2}= & \left(lighthouse^{2}+snowflake^{2}\right) butterscotch^{2}\left(\sinh ^{2} butterscotch\, honeycomb-\cosh ^{2} butterscotch\, honeycomb\right) \\
& +2 lighthouse \, snowflake \, butterscotch^{2}(\sinh butterscotch\, honeycomb \sinh butterscotch(1-honeycomb)+\cosh butterscotch\, honeycomb \cosh butterscotch(1-honeycomb)) \\
= & \left(lighthouse^{2}+snowflake^{2}\right) butterscotch^{2}(-1)+2 lighthouse \, snowflake \, butterscotch^{2} \cosh (butterscotch\, honeycomb+butterscotch(1-honeycomb)) \\
= & butterscotch^{2}\left(-lighthouse^{2}-snowflake^{2}+2 lighthouse \, snowflake \cosh butterscotch\right)
\end{aligned}
\]
so \( \frac{d^{2}}{d honeycomb^{2}} \ln marshmallow \) has constant sign for \( honeycomb \in(0,1) \), and the following are equivalent:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d^{2}}{d honeycomb^{2}} \ln marshmallow & \leq 0 \quad \text { for } honeycomb \in(0,1) \\
-lighthouse^{2}-snowflake^{2}+2 lighthouse \, snowflake \cosh butterscotch & \leq 0 \\
-lighthouse^{2}-snowflake^{2}+lighthouse \, snowflake e^{butterscotch}+lighthouse \, snowflake e^{-butterscotch} & \leq 0 \\
-\left(lighthouse-e^{butterscotch} snowflake\right)\left(lighthouse-e^{-butterscotch} snowflake\right) & \leq 0 \\
-\left(lighthouse-e^{butterscotch} snowflake\right) & \leq 0 \quad(\text { since } butterscotch>0 \text { and } lighthouse \geq snowflake>0) \\
butterscotch & \leq \ln (lighthouse / snowflake) .
\end{aligned}
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution 1. We will show that \( smallest=|\ln (negativeone / negativetwo)| \) by proving that the inequality is satisfied if and only if \( 0<|stationary| \leq|\ln (negativeone / negativetwo)| \). The right-hand side is an even function of \( stationary \); hence it suffices to consider \( stationary>0 \). Replacing \( constantval \) by \( 1-constantval \) and interchanging \( negativeone \) and \( negativetwo \) preserves the inequality, so we may assume \( negativeone \geq negativetwo \).

We will show that for each \( constantval \in(0,1) \), the function
\[
entropyfun(stationary)=negativeone \frac{\sinh stationary constantval}{\sinh stationary}+negativetwo \frac{\sinh stationary(1-constantval)}{\sinh stationary}-negativeone^{constantval} negativetwo^{1-constantval}
\]
of \( stationary \) is decreasing for \( stationary>0 \). For this, it suffices to show that for each \( omegafactor \in(0,1) \), the function
\[
disorder(stationary)=\frac{\sinh omegafactor stationary}{\sinh stationary}
\]
is decreasing for \( stationary>0 \), since \( entropyfun(stationary) \) is a constant plus a sum of positive multiples of two such functions \( disorder \). Differentiating with respect to \( stationary \) yields
\[
disorder^{\prime}(stationary)=\frac{omegafactor \cosh (omegafactor stationary) \sinh stationary-\sinh (omegafactor stationary) \cosh stationary}{\sinh ^{2} stationary}
\]
which for \( stationary>0 \) is negative if and only if the value of the function
\[
chaosmeter(stationary)=omegafactor \tanh stationary-\tanh (omegafactor stationary)
\]
is negative. The negativity of \( chaosmeter(stationary) \) for \( stationary>0 \) follows from \( chaosmeter(0)=0 \) and the negativity of
\[
chaosmeter^{\prime}(stationary)=\frac{omegafactor}{\cosh ^{2} stationary}-\frac{omegafactor}{\cosh ^{2} omegafactor stationary}
\]
which in turn follows since cosh is increasing on \( \mathbb{R}^{+} \). Thus \( entropyfun(stationary) \) is decreasing for \( stationary>0 \).

If \( negativeone>negativetwo \) then \( entropyfun(stationary) \) is zero at \( stationary=\ln (negativeone / negativetwo) \). If \( negativeone=negativetwo \) then \( \lim _{stationary \rightarrow 0^{+}} entropyfun(stationary)=0 \) by L'Hopital's Rule [Spv, Ch. 11, Theorem 9]. Since \( entropyfun(stationary) \) is decreasing for \( stationary>0 \), in both cases we have \( entropyfun(stationary) \geq 0 \) for \( 0<stationary \leq \ln (negativeone / negativetwo) \) and \( entropyfun(stationary)<0 \) for \( stationary>\ln (negativeone / negativetwo) \), as desired.

Solution 2. As before, we may assume \( stationary>0 \) and \( negativeone \geq negativetwo \). Taking \( \ln \) of each side and rearranging, we find that the inequality is equivalent to
\[
constantval(\ln (negativeone \sinh stationary))+(1-constantval)(\ln (negativetwo \sinh stationary)) \leq \ln (negativeone \sinh stationary constantval+negativetwo \sinh stationary(1-constantval)),
\]
which, if we define
\[
disorder(constantval)=negativeone \sinh stationary constantval+negativetwo \sinh stationary(1-constantval),
\]
can be written as
\[
constantval \ln disorder(1)+(1-constantval) \ln disorder(0) \leq \ln disorder(constantval)
\]

This will hold if \( stationary \) is such that \( \ln disorder(constantval) \) is concave for \( constantval \in(0,1) \), and will fail if \( \ln disorder(constantval) \) is strictly convex on \( (0,1) \).

We compute
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d}{d constantval} \ln disorder=& \frac{disorder^{\prime}}{disorder} \\
\frac{d^{2}}{d constantval^{2}} \ln disorder=& \frac{disorder disorder^{\prime \prime}-\left(disorder^{\prime}\right)^{2}}{disorder^{2}} \\
disorder^{\prime}=& negativeone stationary \cosh stationary constantval-negativetwo stationary \cosh stationary(1-constantval) \\
disorder^{\prime \prime}=& negativeone stationary^{2} \sinh stationary constantval+negativetwo stationary^{2} \sinh stationary(1-constantval) \\
disorder disorder^{\prime \prime}-\left(disorder^{\prime}\right)^{2}=& \left(negativeone^{2}+negativetwo^{2}\right) stationary^{2}\left(\sinh ^{2} stationary constantval-\cosh ^{2} stationary constantval\right) \\
& +2 negativeone negativetwo stationary^{2}(\sinh stationary constantval \sinh stationary(1-constantval)+\cosh stationary constantval \cosh stationary(1-constantval)) \\
=& \left(negativeone^{2}+negativetwo^{2}\right) stationary^{2}(-1)+2 negativeone negativetwo stationary^{2} \cosh (stationary constantval+stationary(1-constantval)) \\
=& stationary^{2}\left(-negativeone^{2}-negativetwo^{2}+2 negativeone negativetwo \cosh stationary\right)
\end{aligned}
\]
o \( \frac{d^{2}}{d constantval^{2}} \ln disorder \) has constant sign for \( constantval \in(0,1) \), and the following are equivalent:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d^{2}}{d constantval^{2}} \ln disorder & \leq 0 \quad \text { for } constantval \in(0,1) \\
-negativeone^{2}-negativetwo^{2}+2 negativeone negativetwo \cosh stationary & \leq 0 \\
-negativeone^{2}-negativetwo^{2}+negativeone negativetwo e^{stationary}+negativeone negativetwo e^{-stationary} & \leq 0 \\
-\left(negativeone-e^{stationary} negativetwo\right)\left(negativeone-e^{-stationary} negativetwo\right) & \leq 0 \\
-\left(negativeone-e^{stationary} negativetwo\right) & \leq 0 \quad(\text { since } stationary>0 \text { and } negativeone \geq negativetwo>0) \\
stationary & \leq \ln (negativeone / negativetwo) .
\end{aligned}
\]

garbled_string.question:
Let $qzxwvtnp$ and $hjgrksla$ be positive numbers. Find the largest number $vbnmtrwe$, in
terms of $qzxwvtnp$ and $hjgrksla$, such that
\[
qzxwvtnp^{mnbvcxzl} hjgrksla^{1-mnbvcxzl} \leq qzxwvtnp \frac{\sinh asdfghjk mnbvcxzl}{\sinh asdfghjk} + hjgrksla \frac{\sinh asdfghjk(1-mnbvcxzl)}{\sinh asdfghjk}
\]
for all $asdfghjk$ with $0 < |asdfghjk| \leq vbnmtrwe$ and for all $mnbvcxzl$, $0 < mnbvcxzl < 1$. (Note:
$\sinh asdfghjk = (e^{asdfghjk} - e^{-asdfghjk})/2$.)
garbled_string.solution:
Solution 1. We will show that \( vbnmtrwe=|\ln (qzxwvtnp / hjgrksla)| \) by proving that the inequality is satisfied if and only if \( 0<|asdfghjk| \leq|\ln (qzxwvtnp / hjgrksla)| \). The right-hand side is an even function of \( asdfghjk \); hence it suffices to consider \( asdfghjk>0 \). Replacing \( mnbvcxzl \) by \( 1-mnbvcxzl \) and interchanging \( qzxwvtnp \) and \( hjgrksla \) preserves the inequality, so we may assume \( qzxwvtnp \geq hjgrksla \).

We will show that for each \( mnbvcxzl \in(0,1) \), the function
\[
poiulkjh(asdfghjk)=qzxwvtnp \frac{\sinh asdfghjk mnbvcxzl}{\sinh asdfghjk}+hjgrksla \frac{\sinh asdfghjk(1-mnbvcxzl)}{\sinh asdfghjk}-qzxwvtnp^{mnbvcxzl} hjgrksla^{1-mnbvcxzl}
\]
of \( asdfghjk \) is decreasing for \( asdfghjk>0 \). For this, it suffices to show that for each \( \plmoknij \in(0,1) \), the function
\[
qazxswed(asdfghjk)=\frac{\sinh \plmoknij asdfghjk}{\sinh asdfghjk}
\]
is decreasing for \( asdfghjk>0 \), since \( poiulkjh(asdfghjk) \) is a constant plus a sum of positive multiples of two such functions \( qazxswed \). Differentiating with respect to \( asdfghjk \) yields
\[
qazxswed^{\prime}(asdfghjk)=\frac{\plmoknij \cosh (\plmoknij asdfghjk) \sinh asdfghjk-\sinh (\plmoknij asdfghjk) \cosh asdfghjk}{\sinh ^{2} asdfghjk}
\]
which for \( asdfghjk>0 \) is negative if and only if the value of the function
\[
rfvbgtky(asdfghjk)=\plmoknij \tanh asdfghjk-\tanh (\plmoknij asdfghjk)
\]
is negative. The negativity of \( rfvbgtky(asdfghjk) \) for \( asdfghjk>0 \) follows from \( rfvbgtky(0)=0 \) and the negativity of
\[
rfvbgtky^{\prime}(asdfghjk)=\frac{\plmoknij}{\cosh ^{2} asdfghjk}-\frac{\plmoknij}{\cosh ^{2} \plmoknij asdfghjk}
\]
which in turn follows since cosh is increasing on \( \mathbb{R}^{+} \). Thus \( poiulkjh(asdfghjk) \) is decreasing for \( asdfghjk>0 \).

If \( qzxwvtnp>hjgrksla \) then \( poiulkjh(asdfghjk) \) is zero at \( asdfghjk=\ln (qzxwvtnp / hjgrksla) \). If \( qzxwvtnp=hjgrksla \) then \( \lim _{asdfghjk \rightarrow 0^{+}} poiulkjh(asdfghjk)=0 \) by L'H\'ospital's Rule [Spv, Ch. 11, Theorem 9]. Since \( poiulkjh(asdfghjk) \) is decreasing for \( asdfghjk>0 \), in both cases we have \( poiulkjh(asdfghjk) \geq 0 \) for \( 0<asdfghjk \leq \ln (qzxwvtnp / hjgrksla) \) and \( poiulkjh(asdfghjk)<0 \) for \( asdfghjk>\ln (qzxwvtnp / hjgrksla) \), as desired.

Solution 2. As before, we may assume \( asdfghjk>0 \) and \( qzxwvtnp \geq hjgrksla \). Taking \( \ln \) of each side and rearranging, we find that the inequality is equivalent to
\[
mnbvcxzl(\ln (qzxwvtnp \sinh asdfghjk))+(1-mnbvcxzl)(\ln (hjgrksla \sinh asdfghjk)) \leq \ln (qzxwvtnp \sinh asdfghjk mnbvcxzl+hjgrksla \sinh asdfghjk(1-mnbvcxzl)),
\]
which, if we define
\[
qazxswed(mnbvcxzl)=qzxwvtnp \sinh asdfghjk mnbvcxzl+hjgrksla \sinh asdfghjk(1-mnbvcxzl),
\]
can be written as
\[
mnbvcxzl \ln qazxswed(1)+(1-mnbvcxzl) \ln qazxswed(0) \leq \ln qazxswed(mnbvcxzl)
\]

This will hold if \( asdfghjk \) is such that \( \ln qazxswed(mnbvcxzl) \) is concave for \( mnbvcxzl \in(0,1) \), and will fail if \( \ln qazxswed(mnbvcxzl) \) is strictly convex on \( (0,1) \).

We compute
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d}{d mnbvcxzl} \ln qazxswed= & \frac{qazxswed^{\prime}}{qazxswed} \\
\frac{d^{2}}{d mnbvcxzl^{2}} \ln qazxswed= & \frac{qazxswed qazxswed^{\prime \prime}-\left(qazxswed^{\prime}\right)^{2}}{qazxswed^{2}} \\
qazxswed^{\prime}= & qzxwvtnp asdfghjk \cosh asdfghjk mnbvcxzl-hjgrksla asdfghjk \cosh asdfghjk(1-mnbvcxzl) \\
qazxswed^{\prime \prime}= & qzxwvtnp asdfghjk^{2} \sinh asdfghjk mnbvcxzl+hjgrksla asdfghjk^{2} \sinh asdfghjk(1-mnbvcxzl) \\
qazxswed qazxswed^{\prime \prime}-\left(qazxswed^{\prime}\right)^{2}= & \left(qzxwvtnp^{2}+hjgrksla^{2}\right) asdfghjk^{2}\left(\sinh ^{2} asdfghjk mnbvcxzl-\cosh ^{2} asdfghjk mnbvcxzl\right) \\
& +2 qzxwvtnp hjgrksla asdfghjk^{2}(\sinh asdfghjk mnbvcxzl \sinh asdfghjk(1-mnbvcxzl)+\cosh asdfghjk mnbvcxzl \cosh asdfghjk(1-mnbvcxzl)) \\
= & \left(qzxwvtnp^{2}+hjgrksla^{2}\right) asdfghjk^{2}(-1)+2 qzxwvtnp hjgrksla asdfghjk^{2} \cosh (asdfghjk mnbvcxzl+asdfghjk(1-mnbvcxzl)) \\
= & asdfghjk^{2}\left(-qzxwvtnp^{2}-hjgrksla^{2}+2 qzxwvtnp hjgrksla \cosh asdfghjk\right)
\end{aligned}
\]
o \( \frac{d^{2}}{d mnbvcxzl^{2}} \ln qazxswed \) has constant sign for \( mnbvcxzl \in(0,1) \), and the following are equivalent:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d^{2}}{d mnbvcxzl^{2}} \ln qazxswed & \leq 0 \quad \text { for } mnbvcxzl \in(0,1) \\
-qzxwvtnp^{2}-hjgrksla^{2}+2 qzxwvtnp hjgrksla \cosh asdfghjk & \leq 0 \\
-qzxwvtnp^{2}-hjgrksla^{2}+qzxwvtnp hjgrksla e^{asdfghjk}+qzxwvtnp hjgrksla e^{-asdfghjk} & \leq 0 \\
-\left(qzxwvtnp-e^{asdfghjk} hjgrksla\right)\left(qzxwvtnp-e^{-asdfghjk} hjgrksla\right) & \leq 0 \\
-\left(qzxwvtnp-e^{asdfghjk} hjgrksla\right) & \leq 0 \quad(\text { since } asdfghjk>0 \text { and } qzxwvtnp \geq hjgrksla>0) \\
asdfghjk & \leq \ln (qzxwvtnp / hjgrksla) .
\end{aligned}
\]

kernel_variant.solution:
We prove that
d = |\ln(p/q)|
is the required extremal value.

Step 1  (Symmetry).
Because sinh is odd, the right-hand side is an even function of v, so it suffices to treat v>0.  Interchanging p and q while replacing y by 1-y leaves the inequality unchanged; hence we may assume p\geq q and finally restore symmetry by taking an absolute value.

Step 2  (Auxiliary function).
Fix y\in [0,1] and define
  F(v)=p\cdot \frac{\sinh(vy)}{\sinh v}+q\cdot \frac{\sinh(v(1-y))}{\sinh v}-p^{y}q^{1-y}, v>0.
Our goal is to determine the sign of F.

Step 3  (Monotonicity of a basic quotient).
For 0<\alpha <1 put f(v)=\sinh(\alpha v)/\sinh v.  Differentiation gives
  f'(v)=\frac{\alpha \cosh(\alpha v)\sinh v-\sinh(\alpha v)\cosh v}{\sinh^{2}v}
       =\frac{\cosh(\alpha v)\cosh v}{\sinh^{2}v}\,[\alpha \tanh v-\tanh(\alpha v)].
The factor (cosh(\alpha v)cosh v)/sinh^2v is positive for v>0, so the sign of f' equals the sign of g(v)=\alpha  tanh v-tanh(\alpha v).  Because tanh is increasing with tanh0=0 we have g(v)<0 for every v>0; hence f'(v)<0, i.e. f is strictly decreasing.  Consequently, for any p,q>0 and 0<\alpha <1, the linear combination p f(v)+q f(1-\alpha )(v) is also strictly decreasing.

Step 4  (Monotonicity of F).
Both fractions occurring in F are of the type f, so F'(v)<0 for every v>0; thus F is strictly decreasing on (0,\infty ).

Step 5  (The unique zero of F).
Because p\geq q>0 we can write p=q e^{v_0} with v_0=\ln(p/q)\geq 0.  Substituting v=v_0 we get
  p\cdot \frac{\sinh(v_0y)}{\sinh v_0}+q\cdot \frac{\sinh(v_0(1-y))}{\sinh v_0}=p^{y}q^{1-y},
so F(v_0)=0.  (When p=q we have v_0=0 and lim_{v\to 0^+}F(v)=0.)

Step 6  (Positivity range and maximal d).
Since F is strictly decreasing and F(v_0)=0,
  F(v)\geq 0 for 0<v\leq v_0, and F(v)<0 for v>v_0.
Hence the stated inequality holds exactly for 0<|v|\leq v_0 when p\geq q.  Restoring symmetry by swapping p and q yields the largest admissible number
  d=|v_0|=|\ln(p/q)|.

Special case p=q.  Then v_0=0, so the largest d is 0; this is allowed because for d=0 the hypothesis 0<|v|\leq d is never satisfied and the implication is vacuously true.

Therefore the maximal value is
d = |\ln(p/q)|.

core_steps: Exploit symmetry: since the RHS is even in u and invariant under (a,b,x)↔(b,a,1−x), assume u>0 and a≥b.
Introduce F(u)=a·sinh(ux)/sinh u + b·sinh(u(1−x))/sinh u − a^{x}b^{1−x}.
Prove F′(u)<0 for u>0 by showing f(u)=sinh(αu)/sinh u (0<α<1) is decreasing; this follows from derivative analysis with tanh and the monotonicity of cosh.
Note F(ln(a/b))=0 (and F→0 as u→0⁺); with F decreasing, we get F(u)≥0 for 0<u≤ln(a/b) and F(u)<0 for u>ln(a/b).
Hence the inequality holds exactly when 0<|u|≤|ln(a/b)|, so the maximal constant is c=|ln(a/b)|.

slot1.description: Because the RHS is an even function, the absolute–value sign in the range for u is optional; one may restrict to u≥0 instead.

slot2.description: The open interval for x can be replaced by the closed interval; the endpoints x=0,1 give equality and do not affect the argument.

slot3.description: ‘sinh’ could be replaced by any odd, increasing function h with the property that h(αu)/h(u) decreases in u for 0<α<1; the derivative-based monotonicity proof is unchanged.
